text: We perform a robotic-assisted radical completion cholecystectomy. Our patient is an otherwise healthy, sixty-one-year-old female with an incidentally discovered gallbladder adenocarcinoma.
The video demonstrates the trocar configuration, showing the placement of left-sided 11mm and 5mm trocars used as laparoscopic assist ports.

The surgeon mobilizes the duodenum, preparing to dissect to the right of the common bile duct, just posterior to the duodenum. The dissection begins at the inferior border of the porta hepatis to achieve a complete lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon begins by skeletonizing the arterial structures, starting at the common hepatic artery. Lymphatic tissues are dissected towards the liver, and the peritoneal layer of the porta hepatis is divided in a cephalad direction. The common hepatic artery is fully skeletonized, revealing the origin of the gastroduodenal artery.

The surgeon uses the FireFly system on the robotic platform to identify the bile duct, which fluoresces following an IV injection of endocyanin green. The lateral dissection around the bile duct is continued.

The surgeon continues the dissection medially to clear the common bile duct, dissecting along the proper hepatic artery. Lymphatic tissue is dissected upwards towards the liver base, aiming to skeletonize the proper hepatic artery and identify the right hepatic artery. Tissue between the bile duct and hepatic artery is dissected and removed, exposing the anterior aspect of the portal vein.

The surgeon removes lymphatic tissue lateral and posterior to the porta hepatis, retracting the bile duct medially to clear posterior tissue while being cautious of the portal vein. An accessory branch of the cystic artery is identified and divided, and lymphatic tissue between the bile duct and hepatic artery is meticulously removed.

The lymphatic tissues lateral and posterior in the porta hepatis have been removed, and the bile duct is skeletonized laterally. The dissection progresses proximally towards the common hepatic duct, revealing the right hepatic artery coursing posterior to the bile duct.

The surgeon uses the cystic duct stump to retract medially, revealing the right hepatic artery entering the right hilus of the liver. The anterior aspect of the portal vein is also visible. The anatomical structures visible include the common bile duct, portal vein, common hepatic artery, proper hepatic artery, gastroduodenal artery (GDA), and cystic artery.

The surgeon confirms the cystic duct stump using the Firefly system, then divides the cystic artery and excises the cystic duct stump. The excised stump is sent for frozen section evaluation, and the entry of the cystic duct stump into the bile duct is oversewn.

The surgeon places the specimens into an endocatch bag for retrieval from the abdomen. The liver resection begins with marking the planned transection plane, aiming for a two-centimeter resection of liver segments 4B and 5. Care is taken to preserve all vascular structures near the hilum during dissection.

The surgeon notes the proximity of the right hepatic artery during the procedure. A superficial parenchymal transection is performed using cautery, followed by the use of a bipolar vessel sealing device. A saline drip is attached through the laparoscopic assistant trocar to maintain a constant flow over the bipolar device, preventing char buildup on the instrument.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic ultrasonic dissector through an assistant port to perform ultrasonic dissection of the liver parenchyma, carefully preserving the vessels. Small vessels are divided using a laparoscopic bipolar sealing device, while larger vessels are clipped. The ultrasonic dissector allows for precise exposure of vascular structures.
We found the combination of the ultrasonic dissector, the bipolar vessel sealer, and clips for larger vessels to be very effective in the minimally invasive approach to liver resection.

Our patient did well post-resection of her T-B gallbladder adenocarcinoma, which was discovered incidentally after cholecystectomy for symptomatic cholelithiasis. Her liver and cystic duct stump margins were negative, as were all of her lymph nodes. She was discharged home on post-operative day number three. She is now six months post-op with no evidence of recurrent cancer.

The surgeon places the specimen in an endocatch bag for removal from the abdominal cavity. Hemostasis is achieved, and the final appearance of the porta hepatis is displayed, showing a clear surgical field with no active bleeding.
